title: Test Your Understanding: Cross-Cutting Concerns
description: Test your knowledge of rate limiting, observability, security, and CDNs!

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of rate limiting?

options.text: To cap how many requests a client can make, protecting capacity and blunting abuse
To cache responses at the edge

questions.explanation: Rate limiting caps request volume per client so no single client can saturate the system, and it defends against brute-force/scraping while enabling fair tiers.

questions.question: Which HTTP status code typically signals that a client has exceeded a rate limit?
questions.explanation: 429 Too Many Requests indicates the client has sent too many requests in a given time, often accompanied by a Retry-After header.

questions.question: What distinguishes the token bucket algorithm from the leaky bucket?

options.text: Token bucket allows bursts up to a cap; leaky bucket smooths output to a constant rate
Token bucket drops all requests; leaky bucket accepts all
Leaky bucket allows bursts; token bucket forbids them

questions.explanation: Token bucket accumulates tokens so a quiet client can burst up to the bucket capacity, then is limited to the refill rate. Leaky bucket processes at a fixed constant rate, shaping out bursts entirely.

questions.question: Which observability pillar is best for showing where a single request spent its time across multiple services?
questions.explanation: A distributed trace follows one request through every service with timing at each hop, revealing which service caused a slow request. Metrics tell you that something is wrong; logs give event detail.

questions.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

options.text: Authentication verifies who you are; authorization checks what you're allowed to do
Authentication is for APIs, authorization is for databases

questions.explanation: Authentication confirms identity (who you are); authorization checks permissions (what you can access). A logged-in user is authenticated, but should still be denied access to another user's data they're not authorized for.

questions.question: Which are sound security foundations for a system? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Encrypt data in transit with TLS
Use parameterized queries instead of string concatenation
Apply least privilege to components and credentials
Trust all input from authenticated clients

questions.explanation: TLS, parameterized queries (to prevent injection), and least privilege are core practices. You should never blindly trust input, even from authenticated clients; validate and sanitize everything.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of a CDN?

options.text: It caches content at edge locations near users, lowering latency and offloading the origin
It replaces the need for a database

questions.explanation: A CDN serves cached content from servers geographically close to users, cutting round-trip latency, reducing origin load, and absorbing traffic spikes and many attacks. Only shared, non-personalized content should be cached there.

questions.question: Why is consistent hashing preferred over 'hash(key) % N' for distributing keys across N servers?

options.text: When a server is added or removed, only about 1/N of keys move instead of nearly all of them

questions.explanation: With modulo hashing, changing N remaps almost every key, emptying caches and stampeding the database. Consistent hashing places keys and servers on a ring so adding/removing a node moves only a small fraction of keys.

questions.question: Observability (logs, metrics, traces) should be instrumented before an incident, not added during one.
questions.explanation: You can't see what happened during an outage if the instrumentation wasn't already capturing it. Observability has to be in place ahead of time so you can diagnose problems when they occur.
